title: Session cookies vs bearer tokens
description: Server sessions are easy to revoke but need shared state; stateless JWTs scale but are hard to revoke before expiry.
body: Use when choosing or changing how sessions are represented. Server-side sessions store state and reference it by an opaque cookie: revocation is immediate but needs shared session storage. Stateless bearer tokens (JWT) carry claims verified by signature: they scale without shared state but cannot be revoked before expiry unless you add a revocation list or short TTL + refresh. Do not assume JWT is 'more secure' — it shifts the revocation problem. Actions: pick based on revocation needs and threat model; add CSRF defenses for cookies, secure storage for tokens. Validation signals include tests that a revoked session and an expired token are both rejected. Risk boundary: removing revocation capability is a permission-expansion change needing a human gate.
